audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Joshua / Yehoshua northern coalition, Hazor destruction, hardening, Anakim, and land-rest summary, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for northern coalition geography, massed chariots, horse-hamstringing, Hazor burning, no-survivor formulas, spared mound-cities, plunder, prolonged war, Gibeonite exception, hardening theology, Anakim removal, and rest-from-war claim.

verses.restored: It was, when Jabin king of Hazor heard, that he sent to Jobab king of Madon, to king of Shimron, to king of Achshaph,
and to kings who were northward in hill country, in Arabah south of Chinneroth, in Shephelah, and in heights of Dor toward sea,
to Canaanite on east and west, Amorite, Hittite, Perizzite, Jebusite in hill country, and Hivite under Hermon in land of Mizpah.
They went out, they and all their camps with them, a people numerous as sand upon shore of sea for multitude, with very many horses and chariots.
All these kings met together and came and camped together at waters of Merom to fight with Israel.
YHWH said to Joshua, Do not fear before them, for tomorrow about this time I will give all of them slain before Israel; their horses you shall hamstring and their chariots burn with fire.
Joshua came, and all people of war with him, against them at waters of Merom suddenly, and they fell upon them.
YHWH gave them into hand of Israel, and they struck them and pursued them as far as Great Sidon, Misrephoth-maim, and valley of Mizpeh eastward; they struck them until they left them no survivor.
Joshua did to them as YHWH spoke to him; their horses he hamstrung and their chariots he burned with fire.
Joshua turned back at that time and captured Hazor and struck its king with sword, for Hazor formerly was head of all these kingdoms.
They struck every soul/life in it by edge of sword, devoting them to destruction; no breath remained, and Hazor he burned with fire.
All cities of those kings and all their kings Joshua captured and struck by edge of sword; he devoted them to destruction, as Moses servant of YHWH commanded.
Only all cities standing upon their mounds Israel did not burn, except Hazor alone Joshua burned.
All spoil of these cities and livestock children of Israel plundered for themselves; only every human they struck by edge of sword until they destroyed them; they left no breath.
As YHWH commanded Moses his servant, so Moses commanded Joshua, and so Joshua did; he did not remove a word from all YHWH commanded Moses.
Joshua took all this land: hill country, all Negev, all land of Goshen, Shephelah, Arabah, hill country of Israel and its lowland,
from Mount Halak rising toward Seir as far as Baal-gad in valley of Lebanon under Mount Hermon; all their kings he captured and struck and put to death.
Many days Joshua made war with all these kings.
There was no city that made peace with children of Israel except Hivites, inhabitants of Gibeon; all they took in battle.

For from YHWH it was to harden their heart to meet Israel in battle, in order to devote them to destruction, so there would be no favor for them, but in order to destroy them, as YHWH commanded Moses.

Joshua came at that time and cut off Anakim from hill country, from Hebron, from Debir, from Anab, and from all hill country of Judah and all hill country of Israel; Joshua devoted them and their cities to destruction.
No Anakim remained in land of children of Israel; only in Gaza, Gath, and Ashdod some remained.
Joshua took all land according to all YHWH spoke to Moses, and Joshua gave it as inheritance to Israel according to their divisions by tribes. And land rested from war.

translation_notes: The northern coalition is described as a vast population with many horses and chariots, creating a literary contrast between imperial military technology and Israel's smaller force.
Hamstringing horses deliberately disables animals and causes suffering. It may represent demilitarization of chariot power, but it must not be defended as animal-welfare ethics or reenacted.
Hazor's destruction is archaeologically significant and contested; correlations between burned strata and this narrative require caution about dating, causation, and literary memory.
The chapter repeatedly distinguishes livestock and spoil preserved for Israel from humans killed without survivor, exposing the dehumanizing economics of conquest.

Verse 20 attributes refusal of peace to YHWH hardening enemy hearts so they can be destroyed without favor. This theology must never excuse genocide, eliminate diplomatic responsibility, or claim victims were divinely made resistant.
Gibeon's survival remains the only peace exception in the summary, even though that peace resulted in forced labor.
Anakim language belongs to ancient giant/warrior traditions and must not become racial mythology.
The claim that the land rested from war follows a narrative of prolonged annihilation; rest for victors cannot erase the dead, displaced, animals harmed, or surviving communities.
safety_note: Never use Joshua 11 to justify genocide, animal cruelty, destruction of cities, denial of diplomacy, racialized giant myths, sacred plunder, or claims that Divine hardening makes victims responsible for their destruction. Keep Hazor and the northern dead visible, and refuse to call a conqueror's silence complete peace.
